items.question_text: A radar approach may be offered to pilots in what circumstances?
items.explanation: 5-10-1b states a radar approach may be given to any aircraft upon request and may be offered to aircraft in distress regardless of weather or to expedite traffic.

choices.text: To aircraft in distress regardless of weather, or to expedite traffic
Only to IFR aircraft during IMC
Only when requested by the pilot
Only to military aircraft

items.question_text: Does acceptance of a radar approach waive the prescribed weather minima for the airport?
items.explanation: The note states acceptance of a radar approach does not waive prescribed weather minima. The pilot is responsible for determining if the approach and landing are authorized.

choices.text: No, the pilot is responsible for determining authorization under existing weather minima
Yes, radar approaches have no weather minima
Yes, when the controller offers it
Only if the pilot accepts in writing

items.question_text: What approach information must be issued to an aircraft conducting a radar approach?
items.explanation: 5-10-2a requires: (1) altimeter setting, (2) ceiling/visibility if below 1,000 ft or highest circling minimum/visibility less than 3 miles, (3) special weather observations asap, (4) pertinent airport condition information, (5) lost communications procedures.

choices.text: Altimeter setting, weather, special observations, airport conditions, lost comm procedures
Only the altimeter setting and runway in use
Only the type of approach and runway
Only weather and missed approach procedures

items.question_text: What must be issued before starting final approach for a radar approach?
items.explanation: 5-10-2b requires informing the aircraft of the type of approach and the runway/airport/heliport. For surveillance approaches, also specify MAP location.

choices.text: Type of approach, runway/airport, and for surveillance approaches the MAP location
Only the frequency for final controller
Only the missed approach procedure
Only the altimeter setting

items.question_text: What should be told to an aircraft making a radar approach to an airport not served by a tower?
items.explanation: 5-10-2c requires informing the aircraft that no traffic or landing runway information is available for that airport.

choices.text: No traffic or landing runway information is available
The airport is closed
Contact the airport manager on UNICOM
Proceed at your own risk

items.question_text: For a no-gyro approach, what must be done before issuing a vector?
items.explanation: 5-10-3a requires informing the aircraft of the type of approach before issuing a vector.

choices.text: Inform the aircraft of the type of approach
Issue the missed approach procedure
Ask the pilot for intentions
Issue the altimeter setting

items.question_text: After turn-on to final approach and prior to the approach gate for a no-gyro approach, what turn rate should the aircraft be instructed to use?
items.explanation: 5-10-3c requires instructing the aircraft to make half-standard rate turns after turn-on to final and prior to the approach gate.

choices.text: Half-standard rate turns
Standard rate turns
Double rate turns
Quarter rate turns

items.question_text: For lost communications during a radar approach, what time intervals trigger the lost comm procedures?
items.explanation: 5-10-4a specifies: not more than 1 minute on vector to final, 15 seconds on surveillance final, or 5 seconds on PAR final.

choices.text: 1 minute on vector to final, 15 seconds on surveillance final, 5 seconds on PAR final
2 minutes on all approaches
30 seconds on all approaches
5 minutes on vector, 1 minute on final

items.question_text: What should the pilot do if radio communications are lost during a radar approach?
items.explanation: 5-10-4a instructs the pilot to: (1) attempt contact on secondary/tower frequency, (2) proceed VFR if possible, (3) proceed with approved nonradar approach or execute specific lost comm procedure.

choices.text: Attempt secondary/tower frequency, proceed VFR if possible, or execute nonradar/specific lost comm procedure
Immediately squawk 7600 and land at nearest airport
Continue the approach as cleared
Climb to the MSA and hold

items.question_text: If radar contact is lost during an approach and the aircraft has NOT started final approach, what should the controller do?
items.explanation: 5-10-5 requires clearing the aircraft to an appropriate NAVAID/fix for an instrument approach.

choices.text: Clear the aircraft to an appropriate NAVAID/fix for an instrument approach
Issue a missed approach immediately
Vector the aircraft to the nearest airport
Terminate radar service

items.question_text: When should the landing check be issued during a radar approach?
items.explanation: 5-10-6 (USA/USN) requires advising the pilot to perform landing check while on downwind leg and in time to complete before turning base, or before handoff to final controller for PAR, or before starting descent for surveillance approach.

choices.text: On downwind leg, or before handoff for PAR/before descent for surveillance
After the aircraft is on final approach
Only when requested by the pilot
At the approach gate

items.question_text: How many times must position information be provided before starting final approach?
items.explanation: 5-10-7 requires informing the aircraft of its position at least once before starting final approach.

choices.text: At least once
At least twice
Continuously throughout the approach
Only if requested by the pilot

items.question_text: When instructing an aircraft to change frequency for final approach guidance, what must be included?
items.explanation: 5-10-8 requires including the name of the facility when instructing the aircraft to change frequency.

choices.text: The name of the facility
The controller's name
The approach type
The runway number

items.question_text: When must the final controller conduct a communications check with the aircraft?
items.explanation: 5-10-9 requires asking the aircraft for a communication check on initial contact with the final controller.

choices.text: On initial contact with the final controller
At the approach gate
Only if radio quality is poor
After the landing check

items.question_text: What instruction is given to an aircraft on final approach regarding transmissions?
items.explanation: 5-10-10 requires instructing the aircraft not to acknowledge further transmissions after contact with the final controller and while on final approach.

choices.text: Do not acknowledge further transmissions
Acknowledge all transmissions
Report at each mile
Change to tower frequency

items.question_text: When must a missed approach procedure be issued for a radar approach?
items.explanation: 5-10-11 requires issuing a specific missed approach procedure before the aircraft starts final descent when weather reports indicate any portion of the final approach will be in IFR conditions.

choices.text: Before final descent when any portion of the approach will be in IFR conditions
Only when the pilot requests it
At the start of the approach regardless of weather
Only after the aircraft reaches the MAP

items.question_text: For a low approach or touch-and-go, when must departure instructions be issued?
items.explanation: 5-10-12 requires issuing departure instructions before the aircraft begins final descent. Climb-out must include specific heading and altitude except when maintaining VFR and contacting tower.

choices.text: Before the aircraft begins final descent
After the aircraft completes the approach
Only when requested by the pilot
At the start of the approach

items.question_text: When an aircraft is on final approach to an airport served by a tower, what must be obtained?
items.explanation: 5-10-13a requires obtaining a clearance to land, touch-and-go, or make low approach, and issuing the clearance and surface wind to the aircraft.

choices.text: Clearance from the tower and issue the clearance plus surface wind
Only the surface wind
Only the landing clearance
No special action is required

items.question_text: What should be done if tower clearance is not obtained or is canceled?
items.explanation: 5-10-13b requires informing the aircraft and issuing alternative instructions.

choices.text: Inform the aircraft and issue alternative instructions
Issue a missed approach
Vector the aircraft away from the airport
Have the aircraft contact tower directly

items.question_text: What conditions warrant instructing the aircraft to execute a missed approach during a radar approach?
items.explanation: 5-10-14 lists: (a) safety limits exceeded or radical target deviations, (b) position/identification in doubt, (c) radar contact lost or malfunction suspected, (d) airport conditions/traffic preclude approach completion.

choices.text: Safety limits exceeded, position/ID doubt, radar contact lost/malfunction, or airport conditions/traffic preclude
Only when the pilot requests it
Only when the ceiling drops below minimums
Only when another aircraft is on the runway

items.question_text: On a military single frequency approach, when may a frequency change NOT be required after the approach has begun?
items.explanation: 5-10-15b lists exceptions: landing/low approach completed, VFR conditions during daylight, pilot requests, emergency, cleared visual approach, pilot cancels IFR.

choices.text: Landing complete, VFR daylight, pilot requests, emergency, visual approach cleared, or IFR cancelled
Never; frequency change is always required
Only during daylight hours
Only in an emergency

items.instruction: What must you issue before starting final approach?
items.situation: An aircraft has been vectored for a surveillance approach to Runway 27 at a secondary airport.
items.question_text: What information must you provide?

choices.text: Inform aircraft: THIS WILL BE A SURVEILLANCE APPROACH TO (airport name) AIRPORT, RUNWAY 27, and specify MAP location
Only issue the frequency for final controller
Only tell them the runway number
No information is needed before final

items.explanation: 5-10-2B requires informing of approach type, runway, specifying airport name for secondary airports, and MAP location for surveillance approaches.

items.situation: An aircraft reports gyro failure and will conduct a no-gyro surveillance approach.
items.question_text: How do you control the aircraft's turns?

choices.text: Issue TURN LEFT/RIGHT and STOP TURN commands. After turn-on to final, instruct half-standard rate turns.
Issue standard vectors and the aircraft will navigate using ground references.
The aircraft must use its standby compass for all turns.
No-gyro approaches are not permitted.

items.explanation: 5-10-3B and c: controller issues TURN LEFT/RIGHT and STOP TURN commands, and after turn-on to final instructs MAKE HALF-STANDARD RATE TURNS.

items.instruction: What lost communications procedures and time intervals apply?
items.situation: Weather reports indicate an aircraft conducting a PAR approach will likely encounter IFR conditions.
items.question_text: What time interval triggers lost comm on the PAR final approach?

choices.text: 5 seconds on PAR final approach
15 seconds on PAR final approach
1 minute on PAR final approach
30 seconds on PAR final approach

items.explanation: 5-10-4A specifies 5 seconds on a PAR final approach, 15 seconds on surveillance final, and 1 minute on vector to final.

items.situation: Radar contact is lost with an aircraft that is being vectored but has NOT started final approach.
items.question_text: What is the correct action?

choices.text: Clear the aircraft to an appropriate NAVAID/fix for an instrument approach
Immediately issue a missed approach
Tell the aircraft to squawk 7600
Hand off to the next sector

items.explanation: 5-10-5 Requires clearing the aircraft to an appropriate NAVAID/fix for an instrument approach when radar contact is lost before final approach has started.

items.instruction: What action must you take and what phraseology do you use?
items.situation: During a surveillance approach, you observe radical target deviations that make a safe approach questionable. The aircraft has not yet passed decision height.
items.question_text: What should you do?

choices.text: Instruct: IF RUNWAY/APPROACH LIGHTS NOT IN SIGHT, EXECUTE MISSED APPROACH, or climb to/maintain specified altitude and fly specified course
Allow the approach to continue and monitor
Issue a go-around only if the pilot requests it
Immediately terminate radar service

items.explanation: 5-10-14A requires instructing the aircraft if runway environment not in sight to execute missed approach or climb/maintain altitude and fly specified course.
